questions.question: Which statement best matches the definition of a software process in the SPS notes?
options: A structured set of activities used to develop software from concept to deployment and maintenance
A list of programming languages selected for implementation
A test plan used only after coding is complete
A collection of tools installed for developers
questions.correct_answer: A structured set of activities used to develop software from concept to deployment and maintenance
questions.explanation: The notes define a software process as a structured set of activities across the whole life cycle. It is broader than tools, code, or late testing alone.

questions.question: According to the SPS material, why do software processes matter?
options: They help deliver quality software, manage risks, and align work with stakeholder expectations
They eliminate the need for architecture and design
They make documentation unnecessary once coding starts
They guarantee that no defects will ever be found in production
questions.correct_answer: They help deliver quality software, manage risks, and align work with stakeholder expectations
questions.explanation: The introduction slides explicitly tie software processes to quality, risk management, and stakeholder alignment. The other options contradict the notes.

questions.question: Which practice is described as a good investment in the software process lectures?
options: Traceability and formal reviews
Skipping steps to reach testing faster
Treating implementation comments as the full design
Replacing all early reviews with final product test
questions.correct_answer: Traceability and formal reviews
questions.explanation: Koopman's notes say process formality is a good investment and specifically mention traceability and formal reviews. Skipping these steps increases later cost.

questions.question: In the defect-finding comparison from the notes, which activity is described as late and expensive?
options: Product testing
Design peer review
Code peer review
Layered testing within development
questions.correct_answer: Product testing
questions.explanation: The notes contrast product testing with earlier activities and call product testing late and expensive. Reviews are presented as earlier and cheaper.

questions.question: Comments in the code are the same thing as the design.
options: True
False
questions.correct_answer: False
questions.explanation: The lectures explicitly state that implementation is the code itself and that comments describe implementation, not design. High-level and detailed design are separate work products.

questions.question: Which option best defines High Level Design (HLD) from the notes?
options: The architecture of the system, including major pieces, functional allocation, and interfaces between them
The final source code after the modules have already been implemented
A customer-facing acceptance checklist written after deployment
A brief summary of programmer comments inside each file
questions.correct_answer: The architecture of the system, including major pieces, functional allocation, and interfaces between them
questions.explanation: The HLD section describes architecture, component definitions, functional allocation, and interfaces. It is not the same as code or late acceptance paperwork.

questions.question: A team skips design peer reviews because it wants to reach system testing faster. Based on the notes, what is the most likely result?
options: More bugs will be discovered later in testing, where fixes are costlier
The total bug count will stay the same because testing replaces reviews
Documentation effort will drop and overall quality will improve
Subsystem architecture will become easier to validate without reviews
questions.correct_answer: More bugs will be discovered later in testing, where fixes are costlier
questions.explanation: The notes warn that skipping steps to get to testing faster means more bugs in test, and finding defects later is more expensive. Reviews exist to catch issues earlier.

questions.question: A team plans to keep almost no documentation for a system that must be maintained for many years. Which concern from the notes best applies?
options: Implicit knowledge evaporates with the team, making undocumented long-lived systems difficult to maintain
No-paper processes are always better because they reduce all forms of waste
Documentation is only useful before coding and has no maintenance value
Maintenance gets easier when only the original developers understand the system
questions.correct_answer: Implicit knowledge evaporates with the team, making undocumented long-lived systems difficult to maintain
questions.explanation: The Agile notes warn that undocumented long-lived systems become nightmares because knowledge disappears with the team. Documentation supports future maintenance and understanding.

questions.question: A project manager tells the team to focus almost entirely on coding for the first half of the project and to worry about architecture, reviews, documentation, and layered testing later. Based on the SPS notes, which response is strongest?
options: This is acceptable because coding is the main real activity in software projects and the rest can be added once the product works

This approach ignores the notes' core message that architecture, reviews, traceability, documentation, and early testing must happen throughout the process. Delaying them increases late defects and cost instead of reducing risk.
This is ideal for every project because documentation written after coding is always more accurate than documentation written during development
This only becomes risky if the team also forgets to use a version control tool
questions.correct_answer: This approach ignores the notes' core message that architecture, reviews, traceability, documentation, and early testing must happen throughout the process. Delaying them increases late defects and cost instead of reducing risk.
questions.explanation: The lectures repeatedly argue that coding is only one part of software work. They stress design, traceability, reviews, documentation, and earlier defect detection as essential process activities.

questions.question: If a team can reach testing sooner by skipping process steps, the overall project cost will usually decrease.
options: True
False
questions.correct_answer: False
questions.explanation: The notes say skipping steps costs more in the end because more bugs escape to later, more expensive stages. This is a classic software process trap.

questions.question: Which sequence matches the SDLC phases given in the SPS slides?
options: Requirements, Design, Implementation, Testing, Deployment, Maintenance
Design, Requirements, Deployment, Testing, Maintenance, Implementation
Requirements, Coding, Marketing, Testing, Sales, Maintenance
Planning, Budgeting, Coding, Deployment, Architecture, Maintenance
questions.correct_answer: Requirements, Design, Implementation, Testing, Deployment, Maintenance
questions.explanation: The SDLC slide lists those six phases in that order. Other options insert non-SDLC activities or scramble the sequence.

questions.question: For which kind of project do the notes say the Waterfall model is best suited?
options: Projects with well-defined requirements and minimal expected changes
Projects where requirements change daily and feedback must be immediate
Projects that depend on repeated risk analysis in every cycle
Projects that have no need for documentation or validation
questions.correct_answer: Projects with well-defined requirements and minimal expected changes
questions.explanation: The Waterfall introduction slide explicitly links the model to stable, well-defined requirements. It is presented as less suitable when change is frequent.

questions.question: Which process model combines iterative development with systematic risk assessment?
options: Spiral
Waterfall
V-Model
Bare repository model
questions.correct_answer: Spiral
questions.explanation: The Spiral model is defined in the notes as risk-driven and iterative. Risk analysis is the key feature that distinguishes it from the others listed here.

questions.question: What makes the V-Model different from basic Waterfall in the SPS notes?
options: It adds a corresponding testing and validation activity for each development stage
It removes testing so development can move faster
It replaces requirements with prototyping only
It avoids documentation to stay lightweight
questions.correct_answer: It adds a corresponding testing and validation activity for each development stage
questions.explanation: The V-Model is described as an extension of Waterfall with matching test and verification work across development stages. That is why it suits high-assurance work.

questions.question: The Iterative model is a single-pass life cycle with no repeated review or refinement.
options: True
False
questions.correct_answer: False
questions.explanation: The notes describe the Iterative model as cyclical, with planning, design, implementation, testing, and review repeated in cycles. Repetition and refinement are central to it.

questions.question: Which option best defines evolutionary prototyping from the SPS notes?
options: A prototyping approach where the working prototype is refined into the final system
A prototyping approach where the prototype can never be changed once shown to users
A documentation method used only after deployment
A testing method that replaces requirements elicitation
questions.correct_answer: A prototyping approach where the working prototype is refined into the final system
questions.explanation: The prototyping notes say a prototype may either be thrown away or evolved into the final system. That latter path is evolutionary prototyping.

questions.question: A team is building software for a medical device and needs strict validation and early defect detection at each stage. Which model best matches the notes?
options: V-Model
Throwaway prototyping only
Waterfall without testing links
An undocumented Agile approach
questions.correct_answer: V-Model
questions.explanation: The notes describe the V-Model as validation-focused and suitable for high-assurance systems. Early defect detection is one of its stated strengths.

questions.question: A project is large, risky, and needs continuous refinement through repeated planning and risk analysis. Which model is the best fit according to the notes?
options: Spiral
Waterfall
V-Model
Main branch workflow
questions.correct_answer: Spiral
questions.explanation: The Spiral model is presented as best for large, high-risk work because it combines iteration with explicit risk analysis and evaluation in every loop.

questions.question: Two projects are being planned. Project A is a school platform expected to change based on regular feedback. Project B is a tightly regulated system where validation evidence is critical and requirements are stable. Which answer best applies the SPS notes?
options: Both projects should use the same model because model choice does not depend on risk, feedback, or flexibility

Project A fits an iterative or Agile-style approach because feedback and refinement matter, while Project B fits a validation-focused model such as the V-Model because stability and evidence are more important
Project A should avoid iteration because feedback causes scope creep, and Project B should use prototyping only because regulation reduces risk
Project B should use no formal model because validation will happen automatically through coding standards
questions.correct_answer: Project A fits an iterative or Agile-style approach because feedback and refinement matter, while Project B fits a validation-focused model such as the V-Model because stability and evidence are more important
questions.explanation: The comparison slides emphasize choosing a model based on flexibility, risk, and assurance needs. Iterative approaches support feedback, while V-Model suits strict validation contexts.

questions.question: In prototyping, the prototype must always be discarded before the final system is developed.
options: True
False
questions.correct_answer: False
questions.explanation: The notes say prototypes may be thrown away or evolved into the final system. Assuming they must always be discarded ignores evolutionary prototyping.

questions.question: Which activity comes first in the requirements engineering process shown in the notes?
options: Requirements elicitation
Requirements validation
Requirements management
Requirements deployment
questions.correct_answer: Requirements elicitation
questions.explanation: The process slide lists elicitation first, followed by analysis, specification, validation, and management. Elicitation is where user needs are first gathered.

questions.question: Which requirements engineering activity focuses on classifying, prioritising, and negotiating requirements?
options: Requirements analysis
Requirements elicitation
Requirements deployment
Requirements coding
questions.correct_answer: Requirements analysis
questions.explanation: The notes define analysis as the phase where gathered requirements are classified, prioritised, and negotiated. It comes after elicitation.

questions.question: Which set contains elicitation techniques explicitly listed in the SPS notes?
options: Interviews, surveys, workshops, prototyping, scenario, ethnography, and JAD
Compilation, debugging, packaging, and deployment
Branching, rebasing, merging, and fetching
Load balancing, sharding, encryption, and compression
questions.correct_answer: Interviews, surveys, workshops, prototyping, scenario, ethnography, and JAD
questions.explanation: Those techniques appear on the How to Gather Requirements slide. The other options list activities from different domains.

questions.question: In the lab activity, what information is recorded in the stakeholder matrix?
options: Stakeholder roles, their needs or expectations, and their influence on the project
Only the developers' preferred programming languages
Just the final test cases for the product
The detailed source code structure of every subsystem
questions.correct_answer: Stakeholder roles, their needs or expectations, and their influence on the project
questions.explanation: The lab instructions explicitly define the stakeholder matrix in those terms. It is meant to capture who matters and why.

questions.question: Requirements management deals with handling changes to the requirements document over time.
options: True
False
questions.correct_answer: True
questions.explanation: The process slide asks how to manage the sometimes inevitable changes to requirements. That is exactly the role of requirements management.

questions.question: Which option best defines a stakeholder in the context of the SPS notes and lab?
options: A person or group with needs, expectations, influence, or involvement in the system and project
Only the programmers who write the source code
A testing tool used to review the SRS document
A report generated after deployment is complete
questions.correct_answer: A person or group with needs, expectations, influence, or involvement in the system and project
questions.explanation: The lecture and lab materials treat stakeholders broadly, including users, managers, staff, and others affected by the system. They are not limited to developers.

questions.question: A team asks, 'What are the main problems users face with the current system?' Which requirements activity does this question support?
options: Requirements elicitation
Deployment monitoring
Code refactoring
Branch deletion
questions.correct_answer: Requirements elicitation
questions.explanation: The lab gives example stakeholder interview questions like this one. Asking them is part of gathering requirements from users and other stakeholders.

questions.question: After collecting requests from students, teachers, and administrators, a team now has to classify, prioritise, and negotiate conflicting needs. Which phase is this?
options: Requirements analysis
Requirements elicitation
Version control setup
Sprint Review
questions.correct_answer: Requirements analysis
questions.explanation: The notes define analysis as the stage where requirements are organised, prioritised, and negotiated. That is exactly what the scenario describes.

questions.question: A group is starting the K-12 platform project. They must identify the right people, gather functional and non-functional needs, review what matters most, and then produce a formal requirements document. Which answer best reflects the SPS notes?
options: Begin coding first, because requirements can be written more accurately after the system already exists
Start with stakeholder identification and elicitation, then analyse and prioritise the findings, validate them with stakeholders, and document them in an SRS
Skip validation because user feedback usually causes scope creep
Focus only on technical system requirements, since business and user requirements belong outside software engineering
questions.correct_answer: Start with stakeholder identification and elicitation, then analyse and prioritise the findings, validate them with stakeholders, and document them in an SRS
questions.explanation: That sequence follows the requirements engineering process and the lab/project workflow. The notes do not support coding first or ignoring validation and non-technical viewpoints.

questions.question: If the development team understands the product well, talking only to developers is enough to capture user requirements accurately.
options: True
False
questions.correct_answer: False
questions.explanation: The notes emphasize direct engagement with stakeholders such as users, managers, and support staff. Limiting elicitation to developers risks missing real user needs.

questions.question: Which of the following examples from the notes is a business requirement?
options: Improve power outage response time by 30%
Customers should receive real-time outage notifications
The system must send real-time outage alerts via SMS and email
Users shall authenticate using a health authority identity card
questions.correct_answer: Improve power outage response time by 30%
questions.explanation: The comparison slide uses that statement as a business requirement because it expresses a high-level business objective. The other options are user or system-level statements.

questions.question: Which statement is a non-functional requirement from the SPS notes?
options: The MHC-PMS shall be available to all clinics during normal working hours and downtime shall not exceed five seconds in any one day
Users should be able to enroll and attend classes online
The system should allow password reset
Students should receive coding lessons
questions.correct_answer: The MHC-PMS shall be available to all clinics during normal working hours and downtime shall not exceed five seconds in any one day
questions.explanation: That example describes availability and downtime constraints, which are non-functional qualities. The other options describe functional services.

questions.question: How do the notes describe the software requirements document?
options: It is the official statement of what is required of the system developers
It is a design document that should explain exactly how every feature will be coded
It is a deployment log written after release
It is a Git history report used to inspect branches
questions.correct_answer: It is the official statement of what is required of the system developers
questions.explanation: The notes use that wording directly. They also warn that the requirements document is not a design document and should focus on what, not how.

questions.question: Which chapter in the notes presents a high-level overview of the anticipated system architecture?
options: System architecture
Index
Preface
Glossary
questions.correct_answer: System architecture
questions.explanation: The document structure slides say the System architecture chapter shows the distribution of functions across modules and highlights reused components.

questions.question: Functional requirements may also explicitly state what the system should not do.
options: True
False
questions.correct_answer: True
questions.explanation: The functional requirements slide says they may explicitly state what the system should not do, as long as the specification remains complete and consistent.

questions.question: Which option best defines the user story format taught in the SPS notes?
options: As a [user role], I want [feature] so that [benefit]
Given [test], when [input], then [output]
If [bug], then [patch], therefore [release]
When [manager], because [budget], therefore [deadline]
questions.correct_answer: As a [user role], I want [feature] so that [benefit]
questions.explanation: The notes present that sentence pattern as the standard user story structure. It keeps the story anchored to role, feature, and value.

questions.question: Which rewrite best turns the vague requirement 'The app should be fast' into a SMART requirement, based on the lecture example?
options: Process payments in under 3 seconds with 10k users
Make the app very fast for everyone
Try to improve speed if the developers have time
Ensure the app feels smoother than before
questions.correct_answer: Process payments in under 3 seconds with 10k users
questions.explanation: The SMART slide uses a measurable, concrete target instead of a vague adjective. That makes the requirement specific and testable.

questions.question: Which type of requirement is the statement 'The system must support multi-currency transactions and integrate with international payment gateways' in the lecture comparison table?
options: System requirement
Business requirement
Version control requirement
Stakeholder matrix entry
questions.correct_answer: System requirement
questions.explanation: The comparison slide uses that example to show how system requirements describe how the system will meet business and user needs at a technical level.

questions.question: A team is drafting the SRS for the K-12 platform. They need to decide what belongs in the document. Which option best follows the SPS notes?
options: The SRS should mostly contain source code structure and implementation tricks because those are the most technical details

The SRS should describe what the system must do, include sections such as introduction, user requirements, architecture overview, detailed requirements, models, evolution, and appendices, and avoid turning into a design manual
The SRS should be replaced by a sprint board because user stories make all formal documents unnecessary
The SRS should include only business goals and omit functional or non-functional requirements
questions.correct_answer: The SRS should describe what the system must do, include sections such as introduction, user requirements, architecture overview, detailed requirements, models, evolution, and appendices, and avoid turning into a design manual
questions.explanation: This matches both the requirements document structure slides and the warning that the requirements document should focus on what, not detailed implementation how.

questions.question: Because the SRS is a formal technical document, it should explain exactly how the software will be implemented.
options: True
False
questions.correct_answer: False
questions.explanation: The notes explicitly say the requirements document is not a design document and should describe what the system must do rather than how it will be built.

questions.question: What does forward traceability track?
options: Requirements from their source through design, implementation, and testing
Only the defects found after deployment
Developers back to their Git commits
Budgets from the project start to the final presentation
questions.correct_answer: Requirements from their source through design, implementation, and testing
questions.explanation: The traceability notes define forward traceability as following a requirement into later artifacts. It answers what is being built to satisfy that requirement.

questions.question: Backward traceability helps answer which question?
options: What requirement does this implemented feature fulfill?
Which developer wrote the longest source file?
How many users logged in during the Sprint?
Which branch should be deleted first?
questions.correct_answer: What requirement does this implemented feature fulfill?
questions.explanation: Backward traceability traces a built component or feature back to its original requirement. It confirms that implemented work is justified by a requirement.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of a traceability matrix?
options: To map requirements to design, code, and test artifacts so nothing is overlooked
To replace all validation activities with one spreadsheet
To estimate how long a Sprint should last
To rank programmers by the number of commits they make
questions.correct_answer: To map requirements to design, code, and test artifacts so nothing is overlooked
questions.explanation: The notes describe the matrix as a grid that links requirements to later artifacts. Its job is coverage and visibility, not staffing or scheduling.

questions.question: In the SPS notes, validation is mainly about making sure that:
options: The right product is being built for stakeholder needs and intended use
Every requirement has already been implemented in code
No stakeholder ever asks for changes
The team uses only one software process model
questions.correct_answer: The right product is being built for stakeholder needs and intended use
questions.explanation: Validation is described as checking that the software satisfies intended use and stakeholder needs. It is broader than verifying code completion.

questions.question: Bidirectional traceability combines forward and backward traceability.
options: True
False
questions.correct_answer: True
questions.explanation: The traceability notes define bidirectional traceability exactly that way. It supports both completeness and accountability.

questions.question: Which option best defines a verifiable requirement?
options: A requirement that can be checked or tested to confirm whether it has been met
A requirement that is popular with stakeholders even if it is vague
A requirement that is written only after implementation is complete
A requirement that appears in a slide deck instead of an SRS
questions.correct_answer: A requirement that can be checked or tested to confirm whether it has been met
questions.explanation: The validation checklist in the notes asks whether requirements can be checked. Verifiability is about testability and objective confirmation.

questions.question: A stakeholder changes an important requirement midway through the project. Which benefit of traceability becomes most useful immediately?
options: Impact analysis across design, code, and test artifacts
Automatic creation of a new product backlog
Replacement of all validation reviews
Guaranteed removal of all project risk
questions.correct_answer: Impact analysis across design, code, and test artifacts
questions.explanation: The notes list impact analysis as a major reason traceability matters. It helps the team assess what else is affected by the change.

questions.question: Users are unsure whether a proposed interface flow really matches their needs. Which validation technique from the notes is the strongest fit?
options: Prototyping
Ignoring the issue until deployment
Deleting the requirement
Creating a bare repository
questions.correct_answer: Prototyping
questions.explanation: The validation notes recommend prototypes and mockups when stakeholders need to visualize the system before committing to requirements. It is especially useful for interface questions.

questions.question: A requirements set contains vague wording, conflicting statements, and no clear link from requirements to test cases. Which response best follows the SPS notes?
options: Accept the document because validation can wait until the final system test
Improve clarity and consistency, review the requirements for completeness and realism, and build traceability links such as a matrix to connect requirements to later artifacts and tests
Focus only on adding more non-functional requirements because those automatically remove ambiguity
Rewrite the whole document as source code so it becomes self-validating
questions.correct_answer: Improve clarity and consistency, review the requirements for completeness and realism, and build traceability links such as a matrix to connect requirements to later artifacts and tests
questions.explanation: The notes combine quality measures, validation checklists, and traceability practices. The correct response uses all three instead of postponing quality work.

questions.question: A requirement can still be considered high quality even if no one can test or verify it, as long as stakeholders say they like it.
options: True
False
questions.correct_answer: False
questions.explanation: The notes explicitly include verifiability in the validation checklist. A requirement that cannot be checked is not high quality just because it sounds good.

questions.question: Which statement reflects one of the Agile values highlighted in the SPS notes?
options: Responding to change over following a plan
Comprehensive documentation over working software
Processes and tools over individuals and interactions
Contract negotiation over customer collaboration
questions.correct_answer: Responding to change over following a plan
questions.explanation: The Agile values slide includes that comparison directly. The other options reverse the emphasis shown in the notes.

questions.question: According to the Scrum Guide, Scrum is founded on:
options: Empiricism and lean thinking
Big Design Up Front and zero feedback
Centralized version control and release freezes
Late testing and fixed documentation weight
questions.correct_answer: Empiricism and lean thinking
questions.explanation: The Scrum Theory section explicitly states that Scrum is founded on empiricism and lean thinking. That foundation supports inspection and adaptation.

questions.question: Who orders the work into the Product Backlog according to the Scrum notes?
options: The Product Owner
The Scrum Master
The Test Manager
The GitHub administrator
questions.correct_answer: The Product Owner
questions.explanation: The Scrum definition section says the Product Owner orders the work into the Product Backlog. Product backlog management remains the Product Owner's accountability.

questions.question: Which set contains the five Scrum values named in the guide?
options: Commitment, Focus, Openness, Respect, and Courage
Speed, Automation, Testing, Releases, and Velocity
Planning, Design, Coding, Testing, and Deployment
Budget, Scope, Staff, Contract, and Governance
questions.correct_answer: Commitment, Focus, Openness, Respect, and Courage
questions.explanation: Those five values appear together in the Scrum Values section. They support how the Scrum Team works, behaves, and makes decisions.

questions.question: The Sprint is a container for the other Scrum events.
options: True
False
questions.correct_answer: True
questions.explanation: The Scrum Guide says the Sprint contains the other formal events. Sprint Planning, Daily Scrum, Sprint Review, and Sprint Retrospective all occur within it.

questions.question: Which option best defines the Definition of Done in Scrum?
options: A formal description of the state of the Increment when it meets the required quality measures
A list of all future ideas that might improve the product someday
A report used only to justify cancelling a Sprint
A branch naming convention for Git repositories
questions.correct_answer: A formal description of the state of the Increment when it meets the required quality measures
questions.explanation: The Scrum Guide defines the Definition of Done in those terms. It creates shared transparency around what completed work means.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of the Daily Scrum according to the Scrum Guide?
options: To inspect progress toward the Sprint Goal and adapt the Sprint Backlog as needed
To approve the yearly project budget
To replace all other meetings and reviews permanently
To let the Product Owner assign tasks to each developer in detail
questions.correct_answer: To inspect progress toward the Sprint Goal and adapt the Sprint Backlog as needed
questions.explanation: The guide says the Daily Scrum exists to inspect progress and adapt the upcoming plan. It supports focus and self-management.

questions.question: A Sprint Goal has become obsolete. According to the Scrum Guide, who has the authority to cancel the Sprint?
options: The Product Owner
The Scrum Master
Any Developer
Any stakeholder attending the Sprint Review
questions.correct_answer: The Product Owner
questions.explanation: The Sprint section states that only the Product Owner has authority to cancel the Sprint. This is an easy detail to mix up with the Scrum Master's facilitation role.

questions.question: A team says it is using Scrum, but its backlog is unclear, progress is not transparent, incomplete work is shown as finished, and no one adapts after reviews. Which answer best fits the SPS notes?
options: This is still healthy Scrum because Scrum is intentionally incomplete and teams can drop any practice they dislike
This setup undermines Scrum's pillars of transparency, inspection, and adaptation, and it ignores the need for commitments such as a clear backlog and Definition of Done
This is ideal Agile because removing structure always improves flexibility
This only becomes a problem if the team also stops using version control
questions.correct_answer: This setup undermines Scrum's pillars of transparency, inspection, and adaptation, and it ignores the need for commitments such as a clear backlog and Definition of Done
questions.explanation: The Scrum Guide ties Scrum's effectiveness to transparency, inspection, adaptation, and clear artifacts with commitments. Without those, the team is no longer following the framework in any meaningful way.

questions.question: Because Scrum is purposefully incomplete, a team can remove transparency or inspection and still claim to be practicing Scrum fully.
options: True
False
questions.correct_answer: False
questions.explanation: The guide says Scrum is immutable as a framework and depends on its empirical pillars. Removing transparency or inspection undercuts Scrum itself, not just a minor detail.

questions.question: What is version control according to the Git notes?
options: A system that records changes to a file or set of files over time
A deployment tool that replaces testing
A method for drawing system architecture diagrams
A way to schedule Sprint events automatically
questions.correct_answer: A system that records changes to a file or set of files over time
questions.explanation: That sentence appears directly in the Git introduction. Version control is about tracking change history, not deployment or planning.

questions.question: How is Git classified in the notes?
options: A distributed version control system
A centralized testing framework
A requirements elicitation technique
A project budgeting method
questions.correct_answer: A distributed version control system
questions.explanation: The Git slides explicitly identify Git as a distributed version control system. That is contrasted with centralized systems in the introduction.

questions.question: What does the git init command do?
options: Creates a new Git repository and its .git metadata directory
Deletes all remote branches
Immediately pushes all local files to GitHub
Merges the current branch into main
questions.correct_answer: Creates a new Git repository and its .git metadata directory
questions.explanation: The setup slides say git init creates a new repository and more precisely creates a .git subdirectory with repository metadata. It does not push or merge anything by itself.

questions.question: What is the role of git add in the workflow shown in the notes?
options: It begins tracking new files and stages changes for commit
It permanently records changes in repository history
It downloads the latest remote commits and merges them
It deletes the current branch and switches to main
questions.correct_answer: It begins tracking new files and stages changes for commit
questions.explanation: The notes say git add is used to start tracking new files and to stage modified tracked files. A later git commit records those staged changes in history.

questions.question: The command git commit -a automatically stages every already tracked modified file before committing.
options: True
False
questions.correct_answer: True
questions.explanation: The notes describe git commit -a as a shortcut that stages tracked modified files before committing. It does not pick up untracked files.

questions.question: Which option best defines a branch in Git according to the slides?
options: A lightweight movable pointer to one of the commits
A backup copy of the whole internet
A folder that stores only test cases
A fixed label that can never change once created
questions.correct_answer: A lightweight movable pointer to one of the commits
questions.explanation: The branch slides define a branch in exactly those terms. This is why creating and moving branches is cheap in Git.

questions.question: You want to see which files are untracked, modified, staged, or unmodified in your working copy. Which command should you use?
options: git status
git push
git branch -d
git remote remove
questions.correct_answer: git status
questions.explanation: The notes present git status as the command for checking file states in the working copy. It is the standard inspection step before staging or committing.

questions.question: You want to bring remote commits into your local repository for review without immediately merging them into your current branch. Which command best matches the notes?
options: git fetch
git pull
git init
git commit -a
questions.correct_answer: git fetch
questions.explanation: The slides say git fetch imports remote commits and stores them as remote branches so you can review them before integration. Pull fetches and merges immediately.

questions.question: A student joins the SPS group project on GitHub. They need a safe workflow that keeps the central copy clean while they work on their own changes and later synchronize them. Which option best matches the Git notes?
options: Clone the repository, keep main unchanged as the local copy of the central repository, create a new local branch for your own changes, commit your work there, and then synchronize with the remote using the appropriate push and pull workflow
Edit directly inside the bare central repository so there is only one copy of the files
Avoid branches because they make collaboration harder than editing main directly
Use git pull as the first and only command before every change, even before the repository is cloned
questions.correct_answer: Clone the repository, keep main unchanged as the local copy of the central repository, create a new local branch for your own changes, commit your work there, and then synchronize with the remote using the appropriate push and pull workflow
questions.explanation: This matches the workflow exercises in the Git notes. The material explicitly recommends leaving main unchanged and doing personal work on a separate branch.

questions.question: git pull only downloads changes from a remote repository and never integrates them into the current local branch.
options: True
False
questions.correct_answer: False
questions.explanation: The notes say git pull is effectively git fetch followed by git merge of the remote copy of the current branch. It integrates, not just downloads.

questions.question: How do the SPS project notes describe the early development strategy for the K-12 platform?
options: Use an iterative approach with an early Minimum Viable Product before expanding features
Build the full system in one pass before showing anything to users
Delay all user testing until after final deployment
Ignore process model choice and let each team member work independently
questions.correct_answer: Use an iterative approach with an early Minimum Viable Product before expanding features
questions.explanation: The project brief explicitly says the process should follow an iterative approach with an early MVP. Feature expansion and refinement come later.

questions.question: Which set of work belongs to the MVP Development phase in the SPS project notes?
options: User authentication, dashboard, basic lesson structure, basic AI recommendations, and initial user testing
Final sustainability plan, open-source partnerships, and full deployment monitoring only
Only bug fixing and no feature implementation
Teacher dashboards, robotics expansion, and all final scalability work
questions.correct_answer: User authentication, dashboard, basic lesson structure, basic AI recommendations, and initial user testing
questions.explanation: Those tasks are listed under Phase 1 or MVP Development. Later phases expand features, improve recommendations, and handle broader deployment/refinement work.

questions.question: In MoSCoW prioritisation, what does Must-Have mean?
options: Essential features that are critical for the MVP or release scope
Nice-to-have features that can be postponed easily
Items that are permanently out of scope
Features chosen only by the lecturer after deployment
questions.correct_answer: Essential features that are critical for the MVP or release scope
questions.explanation: The lab notes define Must-Have as essential. It is the category for functionality the team cannot leave out of the MVP scope.

questions.question: What does the MVP rubric expect for good version control usage?
options: Consistent use with clear commit messages and a meaningful commit history
One large commit at the end of the project
No comments or commit descriptions so the history stays shorter
Using version control only after the project presentation
questions.correct_answer: Consistent use with clear commit messages and a meaningful commit history
questions.explanation: The rubric names GitHub commits and comments and rewards frequent, meaningful, well-documented commit history. Sparse or unclear commits score lower.

questions.question: Initial user testing is part of the MVP phase in the SPS project.
options: True
False
questions.correct_answer: True
questions.explanation: The MVP Development phase explicitly includes conducting initial user testing and collecting feedback. Testing is not postponed until the very end.

questions.question: Which option best defines the Minimum Viable Product in the context of the SPS project documents?
options: An early version that delivers core platform functionality before later feature expansion
The last release produced after every optional feature has been completed
A document that replaces the stakeholder matrix and SRS
A grading method for measuring plagiarism percentage
questions.correct_answer: An early version that delivers core platform functionality before later feature expansion
questions.explanation: The project brief uses MVP to describe an initial workable version focused on essential functionality. It is the starting point for later refinement, not the final fully expanded system.

questions.question: According to the MoSCoW example in the notes, how should 'dark mode' be prioritised?
options: Should-Have
Must-Have
Won't-Have
Project Goal
questions.correct_answer: Should-Have
questions.explanation: The user story prioritisation slide gives dark mode as the example of a Should-Have feature: important, but not essential.

questions.question: After the team identifies stakeholders and defines requirements, which deliverables are listed in the project schedule for that stage?
options: Stakeholder Analysis, Requirements Specification, and Project Plan
Final Sustainability Plan and Pilot Feedback Report only
Deployment Report and Bug Fix Log only
GitHub Account Setup and SSH Key Registration only
questions.correct_answer: Stakeholder Analysis, Requirements Specification, and Project Plan
questions.explanation: The project schedule links those deliverables to the early task of identifying stakeholders and defining requirements. They appear before deeper implementation work.

questions.question: A team has only the first five weeks to show serious progress on the K-12 platform. They are tempted to delay requirements work and build advanced features first. Which response best matches the SPS project notes and lab materials?
options: Skip stakeholder and SRS work for now and focus on the most visually impressive features so the MVP looks advanced

Use the early phase to identify stakeholders, define requirements, build the core platform pieces such as login, dashboard, and a basic lesson, add a basic AI recommendation component, and gather initial user feedback before expanding features later
Avoid prioritisation because MoSCoW is only useful after deployment
Leave testing until the deployment phase because MVP work is only about feature quantity
questions.correct_answer: Use the early phase to identify stakeholders, define requirements, build the core platform pieces such as login, dashboard, and a basic lesson, add a basic AI recommendation component, and gather initial user feedback before expanding features later
questions.explanation: This answer follows both the project milestones and the lab workflow. The notes frame MVP as disciplined scoping plus early feedback, not as rushed feature accumulation.

questions.question: A Won't-Have item in MoSCoW means the team still intends to squeeze it into the MVP if enough time is left during the same phase.
options: True
False
questions.correct_answer: False
questions.explanation: The notes define Won't-Have as out of scope for now. Treating it as a hidden MVP commitment defeats the purpose of prioritisation.

questions.question: According to the V-model notes, unit testing traces to which artifact level?
options: Detailed Design
High Level Design
Product Requirements Specification
Deployment plan
questions.correct_answer: Detailed Design
questions.explanation: The notes say unit tests trace to detailed design because they focus on individual modules, procedures, or subroutines defined at that level.

questions.question: Which testing level in the notes traces to High Level Design by checking module interactions?
options: Integration test
Acceptance test
Regression test
Product test
questions.correct_answer: Integration test
questions.explanation: Integration testing is linked to High Level Design in the notes because it focuses on interactions and interfaces between modules or subsystems.

questions.question: What do the SPS notes say about product testing when compared with early reviews?
options: It is late and expensive
It replaces the need for design reviews
It is the cheapest place to find most defects
It makes traceability unnecessary
questions.correct_answer: It is late and expensive
questions.explanation: The notes contrast product testing with earlier review activities and emphasize that late defect discovery increases cost and risk.

questions.question: In the V-model notes, what is the main purpose of Software Quality Assurance (SQA)?
options: To check whether the required process steps are being followed
To replace all functional testing with audits
To write the Product Backlog for the team
To stage files before commit
questions.correct_answer: To check whether the required process steps are being followed
questions.explanation: The notes describe SQA as asking whether the process is being followed properly. It is about process quality, not just feature testing.

questions.question: Regression testing is used after a bug fix to make sure the defect stays fixed.
options: True
False
questions.correct_answer: True
questions.explanation: The notes define regression testing as testing after a bug fix to make sure the bug remains gone and no new breakage was introduced.

questions.question: Which option best defines acceptance testing in the SPS notes?
options: Testing customer-facing functionality against product requirements
Testing internal module logic against detailed design only
Testing commits before they are pushed to GitHub
Testing whether a requirement is measurable
questions.correct_answer: Testing customer-facing functionality against product requirements
questions.explanation: The notes link acceptance testing to the Product Requirements Specification and customer-facing behavior, not just internal module checks.

questions.question: A team wants to find more defects before expensive late-stage testing begins. Based on the SPS notes, which approach best supports that goal?
options: Use design peer reviews, code peer reviews, and layered testing earlier in the life cycle
Wait until final product testing because it is the most complete view of the system
Avoid traceability so the team can move faster
Replace all reviews with one final acceptance test
questions.correct_answer: Use design peer reviews, code peer reviews, and layered testing earlier in the life cycle
questions.explanation: The notes explicitly argue that earlier reviews and staged testing are cheaper and more effective than finding most defects only at the end.

questions.question: A defect appears only when two modules interact, even though each module works correctly by itself. Which test level best targets this problem according to the SPS notes?
options: Integration testing
Acceptance testing
Requirements review
Project presentation
questions.correct_answer: Integration testing
questions.explanation: Integration testing focuses on module interactions and interfaces. That makes it the appropriate level for defects that do not appear in isolated unit behavior.

questions.question: A team claims it can save time by skipping most reviews and traceability work, then relying on one large late testing phase to reveal the important defects. Which response best matches the SPS notes?
options: This is efficient because the final test phase sees the whole product at once and makes earlier checks redundant
This is risky because the notes argue that late product testing is expensive, while reviews, traceability, and staged tests help find problems earlier and link them to the right artifacts
This is acceptable only if the team also avoids documentation completely
This is the preferred strategy of the V-model
questions.correct_answer: This is risky because the notes argue that late product testing is expensive, while reviews, traceability, and staged tests help find problems earlier and link them to the right artifacts
questions.explanation: The SPS material repeatedly warns that deferring quality work makes defects more expensive to fix. The V-model and related notes spread quality activities across the life cycle for this reason.

questions.question: If a system passes acceptance testing, unit and integration testing were unnecessary process overhead.
options: True
False
questions.correct_answer: False
questions.explanation: The notes separate test levels for different purposes. Passing a late customer-facing test does not make earlier module and interaction testing unnecessary.

questions.question: What does the stakeholder matrix in the SPS lab ask teams to record?
options: Stakeholder roles, their needs or expectations, and their influence on the project
Only each team member's coding language preference
The final test report and deployment log
Only the system architecture and database schema
questions.correct_answer: Stakeholder roles, their needs or expectations, and their influence on the project
questions.explanation: The lab instructions describe the stakeholder matrix using those exact kinds of entries. It is meant to organize who matters and what they care about.

questions.question: Which of the following is explicitly listed in the SPS notes as a requirements elicitation technique?
options: Workshops
Code minification
Branch deletion
Binary packaging
questions.correct_answer: Workshops
questions.explanation: The elicitation slide lists interviews, surveys, workshops, prototyping, scenario, ethnography, and JAD. The other options are unrelated to eliciting requirements.

questions.question: In the patient records stakeholder example, who is responsible for installing and maintaining the system?
options: IT staff
Patients
Receptionists
Ethics managers
questions.correct_answer: IT staff
questions.explanation: The stakeholder example in the requirements engineering notes explicitly includes IT staff as the group responsible for installation and maintenance.

questions.question: Which question comes directly from the SPS lab's example stakeholder interview prompts?
options: What features would improve user engagement and usability?
Which merge algorithm does Git choose automatically?
How many Scrum Masters should one Sprint have?
What syntax highlights the glossary chapter?
questions.correct_answer: What features would improve user engagement and usability?
questions.explanation: That wording appears in the lab's example questions for stakeholder interviews. It is used to guide elicitation around real user needs.

questions.question: Stakeholder analysis is useful because different stakeholders can have different needs and influence on the project.
options: True
False
questions.correct_answer: True
questions.explanation: The stakeholder matrix and examples in the notes assume that different groups bring different expectations, constraints, and priorities to the project.

questions.question: Which option best defines elicitation in requirements engineering?
options: The activity of gathering information about what users and stakeholders need from the system
The activity of translating source code into diagrams after deployment
The activity of deleting low-priority requirements from the backlog
The activity of compressing project files for submission
questions.correct_answer: The activity of gathering information about what users and stakeholders need from the system
questions.explanation: The notes describe elicitation as the part of requirements engineering concerned with discovering required services, concerns, and expectations from stakeholders.

questions.question: A team begins its K-12 platform work by listing students, teachers, parents, administrators, and IT staff, then recording their roles and project influence. Which SPS deliverable are they creating first?
options: Stakeholder matrix
Regression test suite
Release branch
Definition of Done
questions.correct_answer: Stakeholder matrix
questions.explanation: That activity matches the lab's stakeholder analysis deliverable exactly. It is meant to organize stakeholder roles, needs, and influence before deeper requirements work.

questions.question: During elicitation for a records system, the team asks about current practice, legal restrictions, security issues, costs, and timescale. In the SPS notes, what is the purpose of such broad questioning?
options: To gather multiple perspectives and constraints before drafting and reviewing requirements
To replace later validation entirely
To avoid talking to end users directly
To produce a finished design immediately
questions.correct_answer: To gather multiple perspectives and constraints before drafting and reviewing requirements
questions.explanation: The patient records example shows elicitation as more than just feature collection. It gathers legal, operational, security, cost, and timing concerns before documentation is reviewed.

questions.question: A team decides that speaking to only one stakeholder group will save time, so it interviews only administrators and assumes teachers and students want the same things. Which response best matches the SPS notes?
options: This is acceptable because high-influence stakeholders automatically represent all other users
This is risky because stakeholder analysis exists precisely to capture different roles, expectations, and influence. Ignoring other groups can leave important needs and constraints undiscovered.
This is ideal if the administrators promise to review the final code later
This makes validation unnecessary because fewer opinions create fewer conflicts
questions.correct_answer: This is risky because stakeholder analysis exists precisely to capture different roles, expectations, and influence. Ignoring other groups can leave important needs and constraints undiscovered.
questions.explanation: The SPS lab and lecture notes repeatedly emphasize identifying multiple stakeholder groups because they do not all need the same things. Cutting that step creates blind spots in the requirements.

questions.question: Because stakeholders often disagree, the safest elicitation strategy is to avoid interviewing them directly and infer the requirements from the development team instead.
options: True
False
questions.correct_answer: False
questions.explanation: The notes treat direct stakeholder engagement as essential. Disagreement is a reason for structured analysis and negotiation, not a reason to avoid stakeholder input.

questions.question: Which quality measure means a requirement should be expressed clearly and unambiguously?
options: Clarity
Portability
Delivery
Branching
questions.correct_answer: Clarity
questions.explanation: The quality notes define clarity as making sure requirements leave no room for different interpretations. Ambiguity causes confusion and rework.

questions.question: Which quality measure is concerned with avoiding contradictions between requirements?
options: Consistency
Availability
Increment
Deployment
questions.correct_answer: Consistency
questions.explanation: Consistency means the requirements should not conflict with one another. The notes warn that inconsistency leads to confusion and misalignment.

questions.question: What does verifiability mean for a requirement?
options: It can be tested or checked to confirm whether it has been met
It is easy to rewrite into source code
It belongs only in the glossary section
It never needs to change after first approval
questions.correct_answer: It can be tested or checked to confirm whether it has been met
questions.explanation: The notes define verifiability in terms of being able to check whether the system satisfies the requirement. A requirement that cannot be tested is weak.

questions.question: Which quality measure asks whether a requirement can realistically be achieved within project constraints?
options: Feasibility
Traceability
Refactoring
Timeboxing
questions.correct_answer: Feasibility
questions.explanation: Feasibility is about whether time, budget, resources, and technical capability make the requirement achievable in practice.

questions.question: A requirement should be measurable if the team wants to make it verifiable.
options: True
False
questions.correct_answer: True
questions.explanation: The notes connect verifiability with measurable statements. If a requirement cannot be checked objectively, the team cannot easily confirm it was satisfied.

questions.question: Which option best defines modifiability as a requirement quality measure?
options: Requirements are written so they can be updated easily when changes arise
Requirements never change after the first version is signed off
Requirements are converted directly into Git branches
Requirements are intentionally vague so they fit many future situations
questions.correct_answer: Requirements are written so they can be updated easily when changes arise
questions.explanation: The notes describe modifiability as making requirements easy to revise as projects evolve. It supports controlled change rather than resisting all change.

questions.question: A team writes: 'The app should be easy to use.' Based on the SPS quality notes, what is the biggest immediate problem with this requirement?
options: It lacks clarity and is not easily verifiable
It is too technical for an SRS
It already contains too many measurable details
It is automatically complete because it mentions usability
questions.correct_answer: It lacks clarity and is not easily verifiable
questions.explanation: The statement is vague and difficult to test directly. The SPS notes push teams toward clearer, more specific, and measurable wording.

questions.question: A stakeholder demands a feature that the team cannot implement within the available budget and current technology. Which quality issue is most directly affected?
options: Feasibility
Completeness
Openness
Mergeability
questions.correct_answer: Feasibility
questions.explanation: The quality notes define feasibility in terms of whether the requirement can actually be achieved within project constraints. Budget and technical limitations make this an explicit feasibility issue.

questions.question: A requirements document contains ambiguous phrasing, conflicting statements, and several important sections that cannot be tested. Which response best follows the SPS notes?
options: Leave the document as it is and rely on developers to interpret it flexibly during coding
Improve clarity, remove contradictions, check completeness, rewrite weak statements to become measurable and verifiable, and confirm feasibility within project constraints
Convert the whole document into user stories only and ignore formal quality checks
Postpone all quality work until after deployment because real users will reveal the problems anyway
questions.correct_answer: Improve clarity, remove contradictions, check completeness, rewrite weak statements to become measurable and verifiable, and confirm feasibility within project constraints
questions.explanation: The quality measures in the notes work together. The best response is to strengthen the document before implementation instead of treating ambiguity and inconsistency as acceptable.

questions.question: A requirement can still be considered strong even if it conflicts with another requirement, as long as both are important to different stakeholders.
options: True
False
questions.correct_answer: False
questions.explanation: The notes treat consistency as a quality requirement. Conflicts must be resolved through review and negotiation rather than preserved as contradictions.

questions.question: Which format matches the SPS notes for a user story?
options: As a [user role], I want [feature] so that [benefit]
If [bug], then [patch], because [deadline]
When [system], because [database], therefore [module]
Given [test], when [input], then [output]
questions.correct_answer: As a [user role], I want [feature] so that [benefit]
questions.explanation: The user story slides give that sentence pattern directly. It keeps the feature tied to a user and a reason for value.

questions.question: Why do the SPS notes use user stories in Agile work?
options: To describe features from the user's perspective and make needs clearer
To replace all project planning and stakeholder discussion
To avoid writing backlog items or tasks
To document only non-functional requirements
questions.correct_answer: To describe features from the user's perspective and make needs clearer
questions.explanation: The notes describe user stories as short, simple feature descriptions from the perspective of the user. Their purpose is clarity around user value.

questions.question: In MoSCoW prioritisation, which category refers to critical features?
options: Must-Have
Could-Have
Won't-Have
Retrospective
questions.correct_answer: Must-Have
questions.explanation: The notes describe Must-Have items as essential features. They are the items that belong in the core scope of the MVP or release.

questions.question: What does Won't-Have mean in the SPS prioritisation notes?
options: A feature that is out of scope for now
A feature that must be implemented before all others
A feature that automatically becomes part of the next Sprint
A feature that only developers are allowed to choose
questions.correct_answer: A feature that is out of scope for now
questions.explanation: The notes describe Won't-Have items as excluded from the current scope. They are not hidden Must-Haves and should not be treated that way.

questions.question: A user story can be broken down into smaller tasks for backlog and sprint work.
options: True
False
questions.correct_answer: True
questions.explanation: The SPS slides show user stories being decomposed into specific development tasks and assignees in sprint planning work.

questions.question: Which option best defines MoSCoW prioritisation?
options: A method for classifying work into Must-Have, Should-Have, Could-Have, and Won't-Have categories
A method for writing only technical system requirements
A testing strategy for verifying non-functional requirements only
A branching strategy that protects main from direct edits
questions.correct_answer: A method for classifying work into Must-Have, Should-Have, Could-Have, and Won't-Have categories
questions.explanation: The notes present MoSCoW as a prioritisation framework used during validation and scope planning, especially for MVP work.

questions.question: A team labels login functionality as Must-Have and dark mode as Should-Have. What does this decision show?
options: The team is distinguishing essential MVP scope from useful but less critical features
The team is treating dark mode as a blocker for initial release
The team is rejecting all user stories in favor of code-first planning
The team is using prioritisation only for documentation style
questions.correct_answer: The team is distinguishing essential MVP scope from useful but less critical features
questions.explanation: This matches the examples in the notes. Login is critical platform functionality, while dark mode is important but not essential for the MVP.

questions.question: Which user story is written more effectively according to the SPS notes?
options: As a user, I want to reset my password via email verification so that I can regain access securely
I want password reset
Reset password maybe somehow soon
Security feature for later perhaps
questions.correct_answer: As a user, I want to reset my password via email verification so that I can regain access securely
questions.explanation: The notes explicitly compare this stronger example against a vague password reset statement. It clearly states who, what, and why.

questions.question: A team has four proposed features for its first K-12 MVP: login, student progress tracking, animated decorative UI effects, and a future social networking feature. Which prioritisation strategy best matches the SPS notes?
options: Treat all four as Must-Have so nothing important is delayed
Prioritise login and core progress tracking as Must-Have items, place decorative enhancements lower, and keep clearly out-of-scope features outside the MVP using MoSCoW
Ignore prioritisation because user stories already solve scope control by themselves
Move all visible UI ideas to the top because MVP should focus on appearance before function
questions.correct_answer: Prioritise login and core progress tracking as Must-Have items, place decorative enhancements lower, and keep clearly out-of-scope features outside the MVP using MoSCoW
questions.explanation: The notes connect user stories to prioritisation so teams can protect essential value first. MoSCoW exists to stop optional work from crowding out core MVP scope.

questions.question: A Won't-Have item is simply a low-priority feature that still belongs in the current MVP if enough time remains.
options: True
False
questions.correct_answer: False
questions.explanation: The SPS notes use Won't-Have to mark work that is out of scope for now. Treating it as a backup MVP commitment defeats the purpose of prioritisation.

questions.question: What is the fundamental unit of Scrum according to the Scrum Guide used in SPS?
options: The Scrum Team consisting of one Scrum Master, one Product Owner, and Developers
The Product Backlog by itself
The Daily Scrum meeting only
A single architect working with one tester
questions.correct_answer: The Scrum Team consisting of one Scrum Master, one Product Owner, and Developers
questions.explanation: The guide identifies the Scrum Team as the fundamental unit of Scrum. It is made up of those three specific accountabilities.

questions.question: How are Scrum Teams described in the guide?
options: Cross-functional and self-managing
Strictly hierarchical and document-driven
Large, fixed departments with permanent sub-teams
Controlled entirely by the Scrum Master
questions.correct_answer: Cross-functional and self-managing
questions.explanation: The Scrum Guide says Scrum Teams have the skills needed to create value and decide internally who does what, when, and how.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the Sprint Review?
options: To inspect the outcome of the Sprint and determine future adaptations with stakeholders
To assign permanent job titles within the Scrum Team
To replace Sprint Planning entirely
To archive old backlog items automatically
questions.correct_answer: To inspect the outcome of the Sprint and determine future adaptations with stakeholders
questions.explanation: The Scrum Guide describes the Sprint Review as the event where results are inspected with stakeholders and future adjustments are discussed.

questions.question: Which artifact is described as the single source of work undertaken by the Scrum Team?
options: Product Backlog
Sprint Retrospective
Definition of Done
Acceptance test report
questions.correct_answer: Product Backlog
questions.explanation: The guide states that the Product Backlog is the emergent, ordered list of what is needed to improve the product and is the single source of work for the Scrum Team.

questions.question: Multiple Increments may be created within a single Sprint.
options: True
False
questions.correct_answer: True
questions.explanation: The guide explicitly says multiple increments may be created within a Sprint, as long as they meet the Definition of Done.

questions.question: Which option best defines the Sprint Backlog in Scrum?
options: The Sprint Goal, the selected Product Backlog items, and an actionable plan for delivering the Increment
A list of all features planned for the entire product life
Only the meeting schedule for the Sprint
A report written by the Scrum Master after the Sprint ends
questions.correct_answer: The Sprint Goal, the selected Product Backlog items, and an actionable plan for delivering the Increment
questions.explanation: The guide defines the Sprint Backlog using those three parts: why, what, and how for the current Sprint.

questions.question: A Product Backlog item does not meet the Definition of Done by the end of the Sprint. What should happen according to the guide?
options: It cannot be treated as part of the Increment and returns to the Product Backlog for future consideration
It should still be shown as completed to maintain Sprint morale
It automatically becomes accepted if the Product Owner likes the idea
It must be deleted permanently from the product scope
questions.correct_answer: It cannot be treated as part of the Increment and returns to the Product Backlog for future consideration
questions.explanation: The guide is clear that work not meeting the Definition of Done cannot be released or counted as part of the Increment.

questions.question: A Scrum Team grows well beyond the size where communication is smooth. What does the Scrum Guide suggest?
options: Consider reorganizing into multiple cohesive Scrum Teams focused on the same product
Add more mandatory meetings until coordination improves
Give all planning authority to the Scrum Master alone
Stop using Sprint Goals so less alignment is needed
questions.correct_answer: Consider reorganizing into multiple cohesive Scrum Teams focused on the same product
questions.explanation: The guide notes that smaller teams communicate better and says overly large Scrum Teams should consider reorganizing into multiple cohesive teams on the same product.

questions.question: A team calls itself Scrum-based, but it has no clear Product Goal, no shared Definition of Done, it treats incomplete work as finished, and it skips the Sprint Retrospective because the developers are busy. Which response best matches the Scrum Guide?
options: This is acceptable because Scrum is intentionally incomplete and teams can omit any parts they find inconvenient
This breaks core parts of Scrum because the framework depends on clear artifacts, commitments, and events that support transparency, inspection, and adaptation
This is only a minor documentation issue that does not affect Scrum effectiveness
This is a recommended shortcut for fast-moving projects
questions.correct_answer: This breaks core parts of Scrum because the framework depends on clear artifacts, commitments, and events that support transparency, inspection, and adaptation
questions.explanation: The Scrum Guide presents Scrum as a complete framework built around artifacts, commitments, and events. Removing them undermines the empirical structure that makes Scrum work.

questions.question: If an organizational Definition of Done exists, a Scrum Team may ignore it and create a completely different one for the same product.
options: True
False
questions.correct_answer: False
questions.explanation: The guide says organizational Definition of Done standards must be followed as a minimum. Teams can add more, but they cannot ignore the shared standard.

questions.question: What is HEAD in the Git notes?
options: A symbolic reference to the branch you are currently on
A command that deletes remote repositories
The oldest commit in the repository history
The name of the bare repository folder
questions.correct_answer: A symbolic reference to the branch you are currently on
questions.explanation: The Git slides define HEAD as the symbolic reference to the current branch. It is stored in the .git/HEAD file.

questions.question: What remote connection is automatically created when you clone a repository, according to the notes?
options: origin
main
upstream-only
default-sync
questions.correct_answer: origin
questions.explanation: The cloning section explains that git clone automatically creates a remote called origin pointing back to the cloned repository.

questions.question: How are remote branches described in the Git notes?
options: Like local branches, but representing commits from somebody else's repository
Temporary files that disappear after every commit
Tags used only for release builds
Encrypted backups of bare repositories
questions.correct_answer: Like local branches, but representing commits from somebody else's repository
questions.explanation: The notes describe remote branches as representing commits from another repository and suggest thinking of them as read-only branches for review purposes.

questions.question: According to the notes, git pull is effectively equivalent to:
options: git fetch followed by git merge of the remote copy of the current branch
git init followed by git add
git status followed by git branch -d
git clone followed by git push
questions.correct_answer: git fetch followed by git merge of the remote copy of the current branch
questions.explanation: The slides explicitly explain git pull as a fetch plus immediate merge of the current branch's remote counterpart.

questions.question: A bare repository has no working directory.
options: True
False
questions.correct_answer: True
questions.explanation: The Git notes define bare repositories in exactly those terms and explain why they are useful for shared central repositories.

questions.question: Which option best defines git fetch in the SPS Git notes?
options: A command that imports commits from a remote repository into the local repository without immediately merging them into the current branch
A command that stages all tracked files and commits them
A command that deletes the remote copy of the project
A command that converts a normal repository into a bare one
questions.correct_answer: A command that imports commits from a remote repository into the local repository without immediately merging them into the current branch
questions.explanation: The notes describe fetch as bringing in remote commits and storing them as remote branches, giving the developer a chance to review changes first.

questions.question: A student wants to work on a change without touching the clean local copy of main. Which approach best fits the Git notes?
options: Create a new branch and switch to it before making the changes
Delete main and rename the new work after the feature
Use git push immediately before editing anything
Work inside a bare repository so there is no working directory
questions.correct_answer: Create a new branch and switch to it before making the changes
questions.explanation: The notes recommend keeping main unchanged as the local copy of the central repository and doing personal work on a separate branch.

questions.question: You are on your current branch and want to integrate changes from another branch into it. Which command from the notes directly performs that integration?
options: git merge <branch>
git init <branch>
git remote remove <branch>
git status <branch>
questions.correct_answer: git merge <branch>
questions.explanation: The merge section states that git merge <branch> merges the specified branch into the current branch.

questions.question: A team member clones the group repository, keeps main unchanged, creates a personal branch, uses fetch to review upstream changes, merges them when appropriate, and finally pushes local commits back to the shared remote. Why does this workflow fit the SPS Git notes well?
options: Because it separates personal work from the central copy, uses remotes and branches as intended, and allows review before integration
Because Git only works if every developer uses a bare repository as their personal workspace
Because pushing should always happen before fetching to avoid remote branches
Because branches in Git are permanent and should never be merged
questions.correct_answer: Because it separates personal work from the central copy, uses remotes and branches as intended, and allows review before integration
questions.explanation: This matches the workflow guidance in the Git lecture, especially around keeping main clean, working on a separate branch, and using fetch for review before integration.

questions.question: Pushing branches to a non-bare shared repository is the safest recommended setup for a central collaboration repo.
options: True
False
questions.correct_answer: False
questions.explanation: The notes warn that central repositories should be bare, because pushing branches to a non-bare repository can overwrite changes.

questions.question: Which early deliverables are linked to identifying stakeholders and defining requirements in the SPS project schedule?
options: Stakeholder Analysis, Requirements Specification, and Project Plan
Deployment Report, Bug Fix Log, and Pilot Feedback Report
Only the final presentation slides
Only the version control history
questions.correct_answer: Stakeholder Analysis, Requirements Specification, and Project Plan
questions.explanation: The project schedule explicitly pairs those deliverables with the early task of identifying stakeholders and defining requirements.

questions.question: Which rubric area directly evaluates GitHub commits and comments in the MVP assessment?
