options: Version Control Usage
Basic Interactivity
Core Functionality
Sustainability Plan
questions.correct_answer: Version Control Usage
questions.explanation: The MVP rubric includes a dedicated criterion called Version Control Usage and describes clear, meaningful commit history as part of good performance.

questions.question: What does the SRS project brief require students to do with the software requirements specification document once it is complete?
options: Save it as a PDF and submit it through the required course channels
Keep it as a private draft and do not submit it
Replace it with screenshots because Turnitin cannot read PDFs
Submit it only as raw source code
questions.correct_answer: Save it as a PDF and submit it through the required course channels
questions.explanation: The SRS project instructions explicitly require the requirements specification to be documented and saved as a PDF for submission.

questions.question: Which of the following is listed as an MVP rubric criterion?
options: Initial User Testing (Execution & Feedback Summary)
Compiler construction accuracy
Database normalization proofs only
Mathematical derivation of all AI models
questions.correct_answer: Initial User Testing (Execution & Feedback Summary)
questions.explanation: The MVP rubric specifically includes initial user testing as one of the assessed categories, together with process model choice, code quality, documentation, and other criteria.

questions.question: According to the SRS project brief, the group project contributes 40% to the final semester mark.
options: True
False
questions.correct_answer: True
questions.explanation: The SRS project instructions state that the project contributes 40% to the final semester mark. This makes the deliverables and documentation especially important.

questions.question: Which option best defines the sustainability plan mentioned in the SPS project deliverables?
options: A forward-looking plan addressing how the platform can continue through partnerships, funding strategies, or open-source contributions
A short list of commit messages from the final week
A replacement for technical documentation
A glossary of all programming terms used in the code
questions.correct_answer: A forward-looking plan addressing how the platform can continue through partnerships, funding strategies, or open-source contributions
questions.explanation: The project brief includes a sustainability plan as one of the deliverables and links it to future continuation, partnerships, funding, and open-source contributions.

questions.question: A team submits strong code but provides weak setup instructions, poor code comments, and thin project documentation. Which rubric area is most directly harmed?
options: Documentation (Setup Instructions, Code Comments)
Version Control Usage
Initial User Testing
Identified Process Model
questions.correct_answer: Documentation (Setup Instructions, Code Comments)
questions.explanation: The MVP rubric has a dedicated documentation criterion that covers setup guidance and code comments. Good code alone does not satisfy that category.

questions.question: A project has useful features and decent UI, but the commit history is sparse and messages are vague. Which assessed area will this weaken most clearly in the SPS rubric?
options: Version Control Usage
Basic Interactivity
AI Recommendations
Core Functionality
questions.correct_answer: Version Control Usage
questions.explanation: The rubric specifically rewards frequent and meaningful commit history. Weak commit practice reduces evidence of disciplined version control.

questions.question: A team delivers a mostly working platform, but its process model is poorly justified, the documentation is thin, user testing is shallow, and the Git history is disorganized. Which response best matches the SPS assessment documents?
options: The team should still score near perfect because implementation quality outweighs all other deliverables

The team would lose marks across multiple assessed areas because SPS evaluates process model choice, documentation, user testing, version control, and other life-cycle products in addition to working features
Only the final presentation would be affected because the rubric does not consider process evidence
The project would automatically pass if the AI feature works
questions.correct_answer: The team would lose marks across multiple assessed areas because SPS evaluates process model choice, documentation, user testing, version control, and other life-cycle products in addition to working features
questions.explanation: The SRS and MVP documents clearly assess more than code. They reward evidence across requirements, process, testing, documentation, and version control as part of the complete project.

questions.question: Late submissions are always accepted in SPS as long as the team includes a strong presentation.
options: True
False
questions.correct_answer: False
questions.explanation: The course outline states that late submissions will not be accepted after the due date and time and will be marked as zero, unless specific supplementary rules apply for genuine reasons.

questions.question: What do the SPS process model notes say about choosing the best model for a project?
options: There is no one-size-fits-all model; choice depends on project needs
Agile is always the best model in every context
Waterfall should always be chosen if documentation exists
Teams should avoid explicit model choice because it limits creativity
questions.correct_answer: There is no one-size-fits-all model; choice depends on project needs
questions.explanation: The Week 2 slides explicitly say no single process model fits every project. Selection should be based on project context and constraints.

questions.question: According to the notes, which model is especially suitable for large, high-risk projects requiring continuous risk management?
options: Spiral
Waterfall
Glossary
Git Flow
questions.correct_answer: Spiral
questions.explanation: The Spiral model is described as risk-driven and suited to large, high-risk projects. Risk analysis is one of its defining characteristics.

questions.question: Which challenge is explicitly listed in the SPS slides on software processes?
options: Changing requirements
Lack of monitor resolution
Absence of branch names
Too many glossary entries
questions.correct_answer: Changing requirements
questions.explanation: The challenges slide names changing requirements along with tight deadlines and resource constraints as common software process difficulties.

questions.question: Which factor from the SPS notes is important when choosing a software process model?
options: Project size and complexity
Favorite text editor
Number of emojis in documentation
Color of the deployment server
questions.correct_answer: Project size and complexity
questions.explanation: The model selection slides mention project size and complexity, flexibility needs, risk management, and time or budget constraints as key decision factors.

questions.question: The SPS slides connect poor planning and process failures with real project setbacks.
options: True
False
questions.correct_answer: True
questions.explanation: The challenges section explicitly says real-world examples show how poor planning and process failures lead to setbacks.

questions.question: Which option best defines a risk-driven software process model in the SPS notes?
options: A model that repeatedly evaluates and manages risk as part of the development cycle
A model that ignores uncertainty and focuses only on coding speed
A model that treats all requirements as fixed from the first day
A model that replaces planning with branch creation
questions.correct_answer: A model that repeatedly evaluates and manages risk as part of the development cycle
questions.explanation: This is how the Spiral model is described in the notes. Risk is not an afterthought but a central organizing idea in the cycle.

questions.question: A project has evolving requirements and frequent stakeholder feedback. Which broad process direction do the SPS notes suggest is more suitable than a rigid linear model?
options: Agile or Iterative approaches
A documentation-free process with no reviews
Only acceptance testing
A bare repository
questions.correct_answer: Agile or Iterative approaches
questions.explanation: The notes connect Agile and Iterative approaches with changing requirements and continuous feedback. Rigid linear models become harder to manage in that context.

questions.question: In the Boeing 737 MAX activity marking guide, which process weaknesses are explicitly highlighted for student analysis?
options: Rush to market and lack of testing
Too many product retrospectives and excessive documentation
Use of GitHub branches instead of subversion tags
Lack of user stories for a dark mode feature
questions.correct_answer: Rush to market and lack of testing
questions.explanation: The marking guide explicitly names rush to market and lack of testing as factors students should analyse in relation to the disaster case.

questions.question: A team is choosing a process for a project with changing requirements, tight time pressure, significant quality risk, and stakeholder concern about oversight. Which response best follows the SPS notes?
options: Ignore context and choose whichever model is currently most fashionable
Evaluate the project in terms of flexibility needs, risk level, validation demands, and constraints, then justify a model choice instead of assuming one model always fits all situations
Choose Agile automatically because it removes the need for robust process discipline
Avoid any formal model because time pressure makes process selection irrelevant
questions.correct_answer: Evaluate the project in terms of flexibility needs, risk level, validation demands, and constraints, then justify a model choice instead of assuming one model always fits all situations
questions.explanation: This matches the process selection guidance in the notes. SPS expects context-sensitive choice and awareness that weak oversight or poor testing can amplify risk.

questions.question: Because Agile is flexible, it is automatically the best choice regardless of project size, risk, or validation needs.
options: True
False
questions.correct_answer: False
questions.explanation: The SPS notes explicitly reject one-size-fits-all thinking. Model choice depends on context such as risk, structure, flexibility, and assurance requirements.
